questions.question: Radiographic exposure devices travel in — or as — Type B packages. Which set of hypothetical accident conditions are Type B packages qualified against?
options: A 30-foot drop onto an unyielding surface, a 40-inch drop onto a steel puncture bar, a 1,475 °F fully engulfing fire for 30 minutes, and water immersion
A 10-foot drop onto packed soil, a 500 °F oven soak for one hour, and a road-vibration endurance test
Crush testing under a loaded truck axle plus a 15-minute flame-impingement test
Only the special-form capsule tests: impact, percussion, bending, and heat
questions.explanation: Type B qualification simulates a severe transport accident: the 30-foot drop, the 40-inch puncture-bar drop, the 1,475 °F (800 °C) engulfing fire for 30 minutes, and immersion. The special-form tests are a separate qualification of the capsule itself. This engineering is why the most likely post-crash survey is normal — but only the meter confirms it.

questions.question: Your transport vehicle is wrecked and your survey meter was destroyed in the crash. Until a working instrument arrives, the boundary you hold is:
options: About 10 feet — enough to keep bystanders from touching the wreckage
A generous default of at least 25 meters (about 75 feet) in all directions per the DOT Emergency Response Guidebook — and much farther if fire is involved
Whatever distance the responding police officers consider reasonable
None — without a meter reading there is no defensible basis for a boundary
questions.explanation: With no instrument, you start wide and conservative: the ERG directs isolation of at least 25 meters (75 feet) in all directions, expanding for fire. Once a meter arrives, the line is set by measurement — about 2 mR/h at the boundary — and shrunk only as readings allow, never the reverse.

questions.question: After a violent rollover, the exposure device is not in the cargo area, and a systematic meter search of the roadway, ditches, and debris field fails to find it. This is now:
options: A property-loss claim to be handled by the insurer within 30 days
A routine event recorded in the utilization log when the crew returns
A lost-source emergency — the licensee must make an immediate telephone report to the NRC or Agreement State under 10 CFR 20.2201 while police help hold the scene and the search widens
A matter for DOT only, since the loss occurred during transportation
questions.explanation: A source that stays missing after a search is lost licensed material, and a radiography source exceeds the immediate-report threshold by orders of magnitude. The licensee — not the radiographer at the roadside — makes the immediate telephone report under 10 CFR 20.2201, with a written report to follow within 30 days.

questions.question: A storage container with lead shielding rides through a fully developed vehicle fire of roughly 800–1000 °C. The device can come out looking normal yet poorly shielded because:
options: Stainless steel becomes largely transparent to gamma radiation after severe heat exposure
The welded capsule always breaches first, leaving the shield empty
Heat increases the source's activity faster than the shielding can compensate
Lead melts at 327 °C (621 °F) — far below real fire temperatures — so the shielding can slump away inside a housing that still looks intact
questions.explanation: Fire rarely destroys the capsule, which is tested to 800 °C — it attacks everything around it. Lead fails early at 327 °C, and depleted uranium can oxidize into particles that ride the smoke. No temperature changes a source's activity; only decay does. That invisible shielding loss is why re-entry waits for a metered survey.

questions.question: Why must firefighters working near a possibly unshielded radiography source rely on time and distance rather than their protective equipment?
options: Turnout gear and SCBA protect against contamination but do nothing to stop the external gamma field
Turnout gear stops gamma radiation but offers no protection against beta particles
SCBA filters remove gamma rays from the air only while the cartridges are fresh
Their equipment protects fully — time and distance are simply cheaper administrative controls
questions.explanation: Gamma is penetrating external radiation: no turnout gear or breathing apparatus attenuates it meaningfully. SCBA guards against inhaling contamination such as oxidized shielding in smoke. ERG Guide 163 has crews fight the fire normally — upwind, with SCBA, keeping time near the device short and distance long.

questions.question: A fire at the job site prevents your crew from taking the immediate protective actions needed to avoid an overexposure. Under 10 CFR 30.50, the licensee's telephone report to the NRC Operations Center is due:
options: Within 24 hours of the event
As soon as possible, and no later than 4 hours
Immediately, before the fire department is called
Within 30 days, combined with the written report
questions.explanation: An event that prevents immediate protective actions — fire is the listed example — carries a 4-hour telephone report under 10 CFR 30.50(a). An unplanned fire that damages licensed material or its device takes a 24-hour report, and the written report follows within 30 days. Nothing ever precedes the life-safety 911 call.

questions.question: A bare 100-curie Ir-192 source (about 0.48 R/h per curie at one meter) is stuck at the end of a guide tube. The dose rate at 10 meters is roughly:
options: 48 mR/h
480 mR/h
4.8 mR/h
questions.explanation: 0.48 R/h per curie × 100 Ci gives about 48 R/h at one meter. Inverse square divides that by 10² = 100 at ten meters: 0.48 R/h, or 480 mR/h — still far above any boundary level. Not until about 100 meters does the field fall to 4.8 mR/h, which is why an unshielded source rewrites the entire boundary map.

questions.question: During a source-out with a collimator on the end of the guide tube, you take a single reading on the near side of the device and it is comfortably low. That reading establishes:
options: That the source has at least partially retracted into the device
That the boundary can safely be pulled in on every side
That the collimator is intact, so the original shot boundary remains valid
Only that the spot where you are standing is low — collimation makes the field fiercely directional, so the complete perimeter must be surveyed
questions.explanation: A collimator or partial shielding throws an intense beam in some directions and a shadow in others. One low reading clears exactly one location. The new boundary is set by walking the meter around the complete perimeter — and the old shot boundary, drawn for a collimated exposure, is now far too small.

questions.question: During a confirmed source-out emergency, who normally places the telephone notification to the NRC Operations Center or the Agreement State program?
options: The radiographer at the scene, before making any other call
The 911 dispatcher, as part of standard emergency processing
The RSO or licensee management — regulatory notification is the licensee's legal duty, and your immediate call to the RSO is what makes the deadlines reachable
The device manufacturer's recovery team when it arrives on site
questions.explanation: The radiographer's calls are 911 for life safety and then the RSO's 24-hour number; the licensee — in practice the RSO or company management — carries the regulatory cascade. The deadlines run from discovery of the event, so a fast, factual first report up the chain is what keeps them reachable.

questions.question: Which event requires an IMMEDIATE telephone notification to the regulator, rather than a 4-hour or 24-hour report?
options: Discovery that an exposure device with its source has been lost or stolen
A source assembly that disconnected from its drive cable, recovered with no overexposure
A crew member's emergency dose assessed afterward at 8 rem TEDE
A guide tube crushed by a forklift while the source was safely shielded and locked
questions.explanation: Loss or theft of a radiography source is reported immediately on discovery under 10 CFR 20.2201 — as is an exposure at or above 25 rem TEDE under 20.2202(a). A disconnect is a 24-hour report under 30.50(b)(2) with a 30-day written report under 34.101, and an 8-rem TEDE falls in the 24-hour band of 20.2202(b).
